description: This scenario tracks the intense enterprise and public sector debate regarding the adoption of Linux versus the proprietary Microsoft Windows ecosystem, spurred by the 'Get the Facts' campaign and the push for open-source government infrastructure between 2001 and 2004. It centers on whether proprietary software remains essential for business stability or if open-source alternatives provide a viable, cost-effective replacement.

key_events.description: Microsoft launches the 'Get the Facts' marketing campaign explicitly targeting Linux total cost of ownership.
Munich City Council votes to migrate to LiMux, causing a surge in support for open-source adoption.
Microsoft CEO Steve Ballmer publicly refers to Linux as a 'cancer' that attaches itself in an intellectual property sense to everything it touches.
Major enterprise software vendors increase integration with Windows, cementing the proprietary ecosystem's hold on corporate desktop environments.

calibration_notes: Useful for measuring how marketing campaigns and fear, uncertainty, and doubt (FUD) strategies influence long-term technological adoption trends versus purely economic arguments.
